content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COfvIIvIISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
. Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fix (916) 322-0886
April 20, 1999
Martha Clark Lofgren
Office of the City Attorney
50 Natoma Street
Folsom, California 95630
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-087
Dear Ms. Lofgren:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the City ofFolsom
regarding the mass mailing prohibition in the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
May the City ofFolsom include in its bimonthly newsletter the text of the mayor’s state
of the city address recently presented at a city council meeting? If not what is an alternative way
for the city to include the information in its newsletter?
CONCLUSION
In its present form, the article “features” the mayor because it singles out the mayor by
displaying his name in an italicized paragraph. To comply with Regulation 18901, the first
paragraph must be in the same typeface as the rest of the article.
FACTS
The City of Folsom prepares a bimonthly newsletter concerning matters of interest to its
citizens. The city distributes the newsletter at city expense through regular mail. Approximately
18,000 copies of the newsletter are mailed to residences located within the city. The costs of
design, production and mailing exceed $50 for each issue. The city has hired a consultant to
Government Code sections 81000- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-087
Page 2
produce the mailing. The consultant determines the content of the mailing. The consultant’s
work is reviewed by the city manager. The mayor does not approve the content of the newsletter.
The consultant would like to include excerpts ofthe state of the city address presented by
the mayor at a recent city council meeting. The mayor wrote the original state of the city
address. Pursuant to her request, the mayor provided an outline of the speech to the consultant.
The consultant, however, did not use the outline, but obtained a transcript of the speech and
edited the speech for the newsletter. The consultant also added information not included by the
mayor.
The mayor did not initiate or suggest the idea that the speech be included in the
newsletter. The consultant, with the city manager, decided to include the mayor’s speech in the
newsletter. Other than obtaining the original outline from the mayor, the consultant h.as not
reviewed the text with the mayor. You have enclosed a copy of the article for our review, which
states, in pertinent part:
“on Feb. 9, Mayor Steve Mikios presented his State ofthe City
Address
Following, editedfor space and clarfication, are
excerptsfrom the mayor’s speech.
“For five straight years, we’ve had a balanced budget, and
I’m proud to say that when we fmish this year, it should be our
sixth. In addition, in each of those five years we also finished with
a $4 million surplus....
“We enjoy one of the lowest crime rates in Northern California.
In my mind, we have an outstanding Police Department that is
committed to being the best there is. For example, the Folsom
Police Department is the first department in the state to offer
authorized laser radar training....
“I am also proud to report that we have fire protection
services that are second to none. We provide a firefighter for
every 1,000 people in Folsom....
“Recreation is a large part of our quality of life, and that’s why I
selected this as another category. Our Parks and Recreation
Department has been recognized throughout many state
awards for a lot of very innovative and creative programs....
“As the last item in my measure ofthe city’s condition, I turn to
Public Works. By early summer, we will be opening the largest
single city public works project in the entire state....
“I have been asked many times if the city of Folsom is run like a
business.... I am proud to say that, yes, we are—and we achieve a
tremendous net profit: our great quality of life.”


•
File No. A-99-087
Page 3
ANALYSIS
Section 89001 provides: “No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public
expense.” The purpose of this law is to ensure that incumbents do not receive an unfair
advantage over challengers in the next election. (Section 8 1002(e).) The broad prohibition is
interpreted in regulation 18901, which provides that a mailing is prohibited by section 89001 if
all of the following four criteria are met:
1.
The item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business, or post office box; and the item is a tangible item, such as a
videotape, record, or button, or a written document.
2.
The item sent either: (1) includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected
officer, or (2) features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing.
3.
Any of the costs of distribution are paid for with public moneys; or the costs of design,
production, and printing exceeding $50 are paid for with public moneys, and the design,
production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted.
4.
More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request.
The city distributes its newsletter twice a month at city expense through regular mail.
Approximately 18,000 copies of the newsletter are mailed to residences located within the city.
The costs of design, production and mailing exceed $50 for each issue. Based on these facts, the
city’s newsletter meets the first, third and fourth criteria mentioned above. If the mailing also
satisfies the second requirement, it will be prohibited. To be prohibited, the city’s newsletter
must either: (1) include the name, office, photograph or other reference to an elected officer
affiliated with the city, and be prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or
concert with the elected officer, or (2) feature an elected officer affiliated with the city.
“Prepared in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert.”
The article in the newsletter includes the name of the mayor and makes numerous
references to the mayor directly with the use of the pronoun “I,” and indirectly with the use of the
pronouns “we” and “our.” (Fisicaro Advice Letter, No. A-96-230; Thorson Advice Letter,
No. A-92-022.) Therefore, we will first address whether the proposed newsletter is prepared in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the mayor.


File No. A-99-087
Page 4
The mayor prepared the speech that will be included in the newsletter. However, under
the regulation, a mass mailing is only prohibited if the mayor is involved in the preparation of
the newsletter. The mayor did not prepare the speech specifically for the newsletter. He
prepared the speech for the state of the city address, which was presented during a city council
meeting. A mailing is not prepared in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with an
elected officer simply by virtue of the fact that the mailing includes a speech that the officer
wrote for some other purpose. There must be some other demonstration of cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert for the mailing to be prohibited.
We have advised that review of a newsletter by the elected officer is sufficient for the
newsletter to have been “prepared in cooperation, consultation, coordination or concert” with the
councilmember. (Whelihan Advice Letter, No. 1-89-063.) We have also advised that if an
elected officer suggests items to be printed in a newsletter, the newsletter will be deemed to be
prepared in cooperation, consultation, coordination or concert with the elected officer. (Stone
Advice Letter, No. 1-89-670.)
You indicate that the mayor provided an outline of the speech to the consultant upon
request, but the consultant did not use it. Instead, she obtained a transcript of the speech and
edited it. In addition, you mention that the mayor did not initiate or suggest the idea that his
speech be included in the newsletter. Rather, this decision was made by the consultant and the
city manager. Other than obtaining the original outline from the mayor, the consultant has not
reviewed the text of the article in the newsletter with the mayor.
Accordingly, we find that the article in the proposed newsletter was not prepared in
cooperation, consultation, coordination or concert with the mayor since the mayor did not
suggest that his speech be included in the newsletter and he has not reviewed the proposed
newsletter. Our conclusion is limited to the facts contained in this letter. Ifthe city establishes a
policy or practice where the state ofthe city address is routinely included in its newsletter, our
advice may change.
Featuring
A mass mailing “features an elected officer” if it includes his or her photograph or
signature, or singles out the elected officer by the manner of display ofhis or her name or office
in the layout of the document, such as by headlines, captions, type size, typeface or type color.
(Regulation 18901(c)(2).) The mayor’s name is included in the first paragraph of the article,
which is the only italicized paragraph in the 12-paragraph article. In its present form, the article
“features” the mayor because it singles out thern mayor by displaying his name in the italicized
paragraph. To comply with Regulation 18901, the first paragraph must be in the same typeface
as the rest of the article.


File No. A-99-087
Page 5
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

The item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence, place of

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May the City ofFolsom include in its bimonthly newsletter the text of the mayor’s state
of the city address recently presented at a city council meeting? If not what is an alternative way
for the city to include the information in its newsletter?

CONCLUSION: In its present form, the article “features” the mayor because it singles out the mayor by
displaying his name in an italicized paragraph. To comply with Regulation 18901, the first
paragraph must be in the same typeface as the rest of the article.
